schools.summary: The Mount of Olives (a mile east) was Christ’s nightly resort for safety, friendship at Bethany, and prayer; Ellicott notes John never names it elsewhere — a hint the section stands apart.
Augustine/Alcuin allegorise: the mount of olive = chrism/anointing (Christ from chrism) and pity (olive = mercy), a fit seat for the mercy he will show (Ps 145:9).
Henry: perhaps no friend dared lodge him, so he went a mile out while his persecutors had homes (7:53).

outliers.note: Flags the topographical detail as evidence that the whole pericope sits loosely in John’s Gospel.

schools.summary: At daybreak (orthros, non-Johannine) Christ returns, ‘all the people’ (imperfect, eager — unlike ch.7’s divisions) gather, and he sits to teach; some words absent from the Cambridge MS, the section’s oldest authority.

Calvin’s classic verdict on the text: unknown to the early Greek churches and perhaps inserted, yet received by the Latins, in old Greek MSS, ‘nothing unworthy of an Apostolic Spirit’ — so to be used profitably.

‘Scribes’ are never named by John (a textual hint); the staged, unlawful arraignment exposes the accusers’ hypocrisy — they cared not for the sin (adultery was rife, the bitter-water trial abandoned) but for the trap.

debates.question: Is the pericope adulterae genuinely part of John?

sides.position: absent from the early Greek churches/MSS, possibly inserted, yet worthy and long received — to be used profitably

schools.summary: The feigned-courteous ‘Master’ and the airtight legal language (kateilēptai ‘caught and still guilty’; ep’ autophōrō ‘in the very act’) show the facts are undeniable, leaving only the sentence as the bait of the trap; the Cambridge MS reads ‘the priests.’

Stoning was not the general penalty (death unspecified = strangling by rabbinic rule) but applied to a betrothed woman (Deut 22:23) or priest’s daughter; ‘but what sayest thou?’ is the real snare (cf. the tribute question, Mt 22:17).

The trap: condemn = usurp Rome’s power of death; acquit = deny Moses. The writing’s sense is unrecoverable (the narrative would have recorded it); guesses range from ‘their sins’ to the bitter-water rite (Num 5).
Calvin: the stooping shows contempt/inattention, not a hidden message; he rejects even Augustine’s Law-vs-Gospel reading. Wesley: silent action to awaken conscience — he came to save, not condemn.

debates.question: What did Jesus write on the ground?

sides.position: unknowable — the meaning is in the symbolic gesture, not the words
a sign of contempt/inattention to the accusers
their sins / an allusion to the bitter-water trial (Num 5)

schools.summary: ‘Without sin’ = free of this same kind of sin (not sinless absolutely); the witnesses cast the first stone (Deut 17:7), so Christ turns the law’s own rule on the accusers’ consciences.
Calvin: Christ presses the witness-law inward; the argument disarms the slanderers’ passions without abolishing the magistrate’s right to punish sin.

The repeated stooping is deliberate — Christ avoids the judge’s office and lets the law written in their hearts convict them, sparing them his gaze so they may withdraw; ‘with his finger’ and explanatory glosses are later additions.

The accusers (the scribes/Pharisees) withdraw one by one, eldest first (more such sins recalled — ‘summoned to judge themselves’); ‘left alone’ means the accusers gone, the woman still amid the crowd (v.12). Lightfoot: ‘little less than miracle.’
None dared stone her on Christ’s terms; ‘condemn’ is judicial (they accused but never passed sentence), and the gentle question is ‘put to her to lead to thoughts of her sin.’

‘Neither do I condemn thee’ is judicial (Christ claimed no civil power) yet names the act sin; ‘sin no more’ (as 5:14) gives a fresh chance — mercy that condemns the sin while sparing the person (3:17); the verse’s apparent leniency led to its omission from lectionaries.

Calvin: he did not acquit but released her, keeping to his office to gather the lost (Mt 10:6) and call her to repentance; this no more abolishes adultery’s punishment than Lk 12:13 abolishes inheritance law.

‘I am the light of the world’ set against the Tabernacles illumination (candelabra, pillar of cloud/fire); a supreme, exclusive claim — the true Light (1:9) of which his followers are only reflections (Mt 5:14); ‘Light’ was a rabbinic title of Messiah/God.
Alcuin/Bede: after forgiving the woman he discloses his divine nature — the Light not of angels but of mankind in darkness. Augustine: follow him to escape the darkness of moral character.
Chrysostom: against the Galilee slur (7:52) he claims to be not a prophet but ‘Lord of the whole earth.’

Calvin: a universal remedy for our natural blindness (no Jew/Gentile, learned/ignorant distinction) — but none come to be illumined till they own their blindness. Wesley: the follower has light unto life everlasting.

themes: ‘I am the light of the world’

schools.summary: The technical objection: ‘no man witnesses for himself,’ the Law needing two (Deut 17:6); ‘they stand in daylight demanding proof the sun has risen’; rabbinic new-moon attestation parallels the two-witness reply (v.18).
Chrysostom: his self-witness is true because ‘I know whence I come’ = I am God of God; Alcuin: he had sent the prophets as witnesses before his incarnation.
Calvin/Wesley: the maxim holds for ordinary men, but Christ, united to the Father, is no private party pleading his own cause (cf. 5:31).

The two-witness rule rests on the imperfection of human knowledge, but Christ ‘knows whence he came and whither he goes’ and so can truly witness alone; the Pharisees, judging by the flesh, can trace neither his origin nor his end.

themes: ‘whence I came and whither I go’

schools.summary: ‘After the flesh’ = by appearance and prejudice (2 Cor 5:16); they loved darkness (3:19); ‘I judge no man’ = not in their carnal manner, nor now at his first coming (3:17).
Calvin: they are incompetent judges, lacking the Spirit; Christ’s judgment, when given, is never after the flesh.
Augustine: John the eagle soared above the angels to the Word — the very Light these carnal judges cannot perceive.
His judgment is ‘true’ in the deeper sense (alēthinē, 1:9), grounded in his heart-searching omniscience and in union with the Father, with whom his verdict coincides.
Calvin: he names the Father rather than openly asserting his veiled Divinity; Wesley: Father and Son are one God, inseparable (14:10), so the judge is never alone.
‘Your law’ meets the expounders on their own ground (not anti-Mosaic, Mt 5:17); the two-witness rule (Deut 17:6; 19:15) — if two men suffice, the witness of Jesus and God surely does.

Calvin: the Son is excluded from the common rank of men, no private party; he distinguishes himself from the Father by accommodation, as the Father’s servant. Henry: knowing Christ is the way to know the Father.

The two witnesses are the Son (who knows his own origin) and the Father who sent him; self-witness is admissible from one with ‘no party ends to serve,’ and Christ’s word is the Ambassador’s, ‘official as well as personal.’

‘Where is thy Father?’ is a derisive sneer (‘you mean Joseph the carpenter’); the reply makes knowledge of the Father depend on knowledge of the Son — to be ignorant of Christ is to be ignorant of God.
Augustine/Theophylact: they understood ‘Father’ carnally (proving they judge after the flesh), the taunt hinting at illegitimacy or Joseph’s lowliness; they asked to tempt, not to learn.
Calvin: their boasted knowledge of God is empty, for God is known only in the face of Christ; Henry: those who will not learn of Christ grow vain in their notions of God.

themes: knowing the Son and the Father

schools.summary: The treasury (gazophylakion, 13 chests in the Court of the Women) was the most public part of the temple, near the Sanhedrin’s chamber — he taught openly, ‘and yet’ none seized him, for his hour was not come (7:30).
Barnes/Calvin: God restrains the wicked who could have seized him with a word, constraining them to hear him openly — the astonishing power of providence guarding Christ till his appointed hour.
Chrysostom: the Jews’ folly — often foiled, unawed by his power, they persist; he teaches the very claims that sting them.

themes: the treasury / Court of the Women

schools.summary: A turn to warning: ‘I go my way’ (death/return to the Father), ‘ye shall seek me’ in the despair of too-late Messianic longing (1:11), and ‘die in your sin’ — unpardoned, for there is no pardon but in him (Acts 4:12).
Augustine: his going is a free, not compulsory, sacrifice, death a return whence he came; Theophylact: he will rise in glory to God’s right hand.
Origen: even persecutors will ‘seek’ him too late; the saying shows he had power over his own death.
Calvin: the gospel breathes life or death (2 Cor 2:16); the wicked will feel their loss when repentance is no longer possible. Henry: he turns from piping grace to mourning wrath.

‘Will he kill himself?’ is a subtle, bitter sneer (suicides held to the deepest Gehenna, where rabbis ‘could not join him’); they grasp he means death yet mock — while Christ bears the contradiction patiently.
Calvin: fearless scorn and effrontery, reckoning his going of no moment; Gill: a Satan-like malice hoping he would indeed destroy himself.
‘From beneath / of this world’ vs ‘from above / not of this world’ contrasts the earthly-sensual with the heavenly (Jas 3:15; Col 3:1); the gulf is heaven-vs-earth, not Abraham’s bosom vs Hades.
Calvin: they reject his doctrine from an innate dislike of God’s kingdom; the earth-bound mind cannot receive heavenly wisdom till Christ forms it by his Spirit — hence faith is rare.

‘Sin’ (the state of unbelief, 16:9) becomes ‘sins’ (its acts); ‘that I am he’ may mean Messiah/Light/Deliverer or the absolute ‘I AM’ of Deity (Deut 32:39; Isa 43:10; Exod 3:14) — to disbelieve it is to die unforgiven.
Calvin: unbelief is the source of all evils because it drives us from Christ and his grace; rejecting the medicine is their mortal disease.

debates.question: What does ‘I am he’ (egō eimi) claim?

sides.position: the Messiah / the one sent / the Light

schools.summary: ‘Who art thou?’ is contemptuous; they miss the ‘I am’ as a divine Name. The reply (tēn archēn) is grammatically hard — ‘what I said from the beginning’ (Lightfoot, Wesley), or ‘the chief/supreme’ — but refers them to all he has already claimed.
Augustine: ‘Believe Me to be the Beginning’ (archē, feminine) — the changeless Beginning that remains ever as it is.
Calvin: rejects ‘I am the beginning’ as an assertion of Divinity here — no such ambiguity in the Greek; even the Greek fathers differ on the phrase.

debates.question: What does Christ’s answer ‘tēn archēn…’ mean?

sides.position: ‘what I told you from the beginning’ (his prior claims)
‘the Beginning / the chief’ — a title of himself
not an assertion of Divinity; the rendering ‘I am the beginning’ is mistaken

schools.summary: He could justly judge them (knowing all) but came to save, leaving judgment to the true Father — who (Lightfoot) has already judged them in prophecy (Isa 6:10; 29:10); his word is only what he has heard from the Father (7:16).
Calvin: he summons them to God’s judgment-seat; the Father’s truthfulness guarantees the warning will stand.

themes: speaking what he heard from the Father

schools.summary: They failed to perceive he spoke of the Father — whether from unwillingness, preoccupation with an earthly deliverer, or judicial hardening; Ellicott: they did not know that the Sender and the Father are one.
Calvin: a stupidity of minds ‘possessed by Satan’ — plainly summoned to God’s judgment yet wholly blind; such blindness bids us walk with fear.
‘Lifted up’ = crucifixion (and ascension, 3:14; 12:32), the Jews its instruments (Acts 3:15); then they will ‘know’ (by resurrection, judgment) — but too late to change the heart (16:8).
Augustine: ‘that I am he’ echoes ‘I AM THAT I AM’ (Exod 3:14); the cross was wrought by hands that should later believe — that even his crucifiers might be saved.
Calvin: they do not deserve more words; yet they will one day know a Prophet of God lived among them — a knowledge that comes too late, in punishment.

themes: ‘that I am he’ as the divine Name

schools.summary: Though men forsake him the Father never leaves him alone (16:32); the unbroken presence rests on his perfect obedience — he ‘always’ does what pleases the Father (Ps 40:8; Mt 3:17).
Calvin: Christ’s confidence that God assists him is the model for all faithful teachers, whose word God makes effectual by the secret power of his Spirit.

themes: ‘the Father hath not left me alone’

schools.summary: Many believed amid the hostility — the same discourse that hardened the Pharisees softened the simple-hearted (‘the sun that hardens clay softens wax’; 2 Cor 2:16); yet this faith will soon be tested (vv.31–32).
They ‘believed him’ (dative, his claims) not yet ‘on him’; abiding in his word is the test of real discipleship — faith born under one sermon must be proved by its fruit (1 John 2:4).
Calvin: it is not enough to begin well — perseverance to the end distinguishes true followers from hypocrites. Chrysostom: ‘if ye continue’ exposes the heart.
Henry: Christ turns from the proud Pharisees to comfort the few trembling believers.
‘The truth’ is the gospel and Christ himself (1:14; 14:6), known by doing God’s will (7:17); it is a power, not a mere system; the freedom is from sin’s slavery (Rom 6:16; Gal 5:1), not political.
Calvin: perseverance is rewarded with fuller knowledge — all of us ‘need new additions’ — which Christ himself works by his Spirit.
‘We be Abraham’s seed’ is the proudest Jewish boast, but ‘never in bondage’ is a palpable untruth (Egypt, Babylon, Persia, and now Rome); they misread spiritual freedom as a slur on national liberty.
Calvin: a confused cry of despisers, ascribing to all what belongs to a part; they leaned on their election and adoption (Rom 9:4) and could not bear to be called bondsmen.

sides.position: the unbelieving bystanders/Pharisees (the murderous sequel fits them)
the ‘Jews who had believed’ (v.31), whose tested faith now fails

schools.summary: Not political but moral bondage: the one who keeps on ‘committing sin’ (present participle) is sin’s slave (Rom 6:16; 2 Pet 2:19) — the slavery from which Christ promised freedom.
Calvin: the sinner is no free agent but a bondslave, however he boasts; only Christ breaks the chain. Cyril: deliverance comes by baptismal grace.
The slave has no abiding place (Hagar/Ishmael cast out, Gen 21:10; Gal 4:30); the Son abides ever — so natural descent from Abraham gives no lasting title; only sonship does.
Calvin: the bondsman’s insecure tenure is the lot of all who remain in sin; the Son’s eternal abiding grounds the adopted believer’s permanence in the Father’s house.
‘Free indeed’ (ontōs) = real, lasting freedom, against the empty boast of v.33; because the Son abides ever (v.35), the liberty he gives from sin (v.34) is permanent and makes slaves into sons.
Calvin: there is no true freedom but what the Son bestows, as sure and lasting as his own abiding; Wesley: a real, entire deliverance into the liberty of God’s children.

He grants their physical descent but denies their spirit; the proof is that his word ‘has no room’ (chōrei) in them and they seek to kill him — Jerusalem now like Galilee (6:60-66); Lightfoot: the drift is toward ‘seed of the serpent’ (v.44).
Calvin: the concession ridicules their pride — what avails the title ‘Abraham’s seed’ to men who rage against God and shed innocent blood?
Henry: where the word has no place no good is to be expected — like a sick man refusing his physician, past hope.
Two opposite ‘fathers’: Christ speaks what he ‘saw with my Father’ (intimate fellowship, 1:1); they do what they ‘heard with your father’ — not the same person, pointing to v.44.
Henry: the two fathers mark the two families of mankind — God and the devil — ‘contrary the one to the other.’
Calvin: an argument from contraries — their fury against his Father-taught word proves they have an opposite father; he guards the gospel from contempt.

themes: children of God vs. of the devil

schools.summary: ‘Children’ means those who share a father’s spirit and works; they are Abraham’s physical seed but not his ethical children, for the true child bears the father’s moral impress (faith, obedience, uprightness).
Calvin: their haughty clinging to a fleshly title, devoid of Abraham’s faith, proves how fiercely they despised Christ’s teaching.

debates.question: Is the reading ‘if ye were’ or ‘if ye are’ Abraham’s children?

sides.position: ‘if ye were’ (contrary to fact) — the AV reading
‘if ye are’ (first-class condition) — the better MSS

schools.summary: A true child of Abraham would imitate him, but they seek to kill the truth-telling messenger of God — ‘this did not Abraham’; Jesus calls himself ‘a man’ (anthrōpon) even as he speaks God’s truth.
Calvin: they are not merely murderers but driven by hatred of God and his truth — far more heinous. Cyril: ‘a man’ refutes those who deny Christ’s humanity.

themes: ‘a man that hath told you the truth’

schools.summary: Christ repeats the charge, still not naming Satan; the Jews leap from Abraham to God and protest ‘not born of fornication’ (= not an idolatrous, spurious race, Isa 1:21) — perhaps with a bitter fling at Jesus’ birth.
Calvin: their false syllogism — Abraham’s seed, therefore God’s children — imagines God bound to the whole natural line; the same error as Papist succession.
Augustine/Cyril: spiritual sonship is by imitation, not birth — as Paul fathered the Corinthians by teaching, so the devil fathers those who consent to him.

God’s true children would love the One who came from God (1 John 5:1); their failure to love Jesus proves God is not their Father; ‘I proceeded forth from God’ implies conscious pre-existence (17:5; 16:27).
Calvin: a child of God acknowledges his firstborn Son — there is no piety where Christ is rejected; to claim the Father while hating the Son refutes itself.

themes: love of Christ the mark of sonship
claiming God while hating the Son

schools.summary: ‘Speech’ (lalia, manner) vs ‘word’ (logos, matter): they cannot ‘hear’ = cannot bear/tolerate his searching doctrine, so they pervert his manner; a moral, not intellectual, inability.
Calvin/Wesley: an obstinacy bordering on diabolical rage; not desiring to do his will, they cannot understand his teaching (7:17).

The devil their father by their works; ‘murderer from the beginning’ (anthrōpoktonos) = Adam’s ruin and Abel’s death (Gen 3–4; 1 John 3:12); ‘a liar and the father of it’ — lying and murder his two marks.
Augustine/Origen: children of the devil by imitation, not by birth — against the Manichean ‘nature of evil’; the first murder was the devil’s ‘evil word’ that slew man.
Chrysostom: he overthrows the greater claim — that God is their Father.
Calvin: their murderous hatred of the truth marks them children of the liar and murderer — by chosen imitation, not a fate of nature.

debates.question: Are they ‘children of the devil’ by nature or by imitation?

sides.position: by imitation/free consent (orthodox), against the Manichean nature-of-evil
by an original evil nature (the Manichean error, rejected)

themes: murderer and liar from the beginning
children of the devil by imitation

schools.summary: The startling reversal — truth itself the cause of unbelief; ‘I’ (emphatic), the truth-teller, against the devil the liar; truth normally commands belief, yet they reject it (3:19).
Calvin: that they oppose only because truth is hateful to them proves them children of Satan.

‘Convinceth’ = convicteth (elenchō, 16:8); the unanswered challenge implies sinlessness (Heb 4:15) and silences all who watched him — though Barnes/Clarke read ‘sin’ as ‘falsehood/imposture’ (opposed to ‘truth’ in the verse).
Calvin: spoken from perfect confidence — no crime dwells in him; Wesley: his unreprovable life confirms his doctrine, so their unbelief is irrational.

debates.question: Does ‘sin’ here mean moral sin (sinlessness) or ‘falsehood/imposture’?

sides.position: moral sin — the challenge implies Christ’s sinlessness (Heb 4:15)
‘falsehood/imposture,’ opposed to ‘truth’ in the same verse

themes: ‘which of you convicteth me of sin?’

schools.summary: The counterpart to v.44: as the devil’s children do his lusts, God’s children hear his words; willingness to hear is the proof of being his (1 John 2:4) — their deafness proves they are not of God.
Calvin: as the Father’s ambassador teaching only God’s word, Christ concludes they have nothing in common with God since they will not hear it.

themes: ‘he that is of God heareth God’s words’
hearing as the mark of God’s children

schools.summary: Malice erupts when argument fails: ‘Samaritan’ = heretic/schismatic (he had challenged their privileges, 4:9), ‘hast a devil’ an old charge (Mt 12:24; 10:20); Lightfoot shows the slur insincere — no Samaritan could stand in the Treasury.
Chrysostom: wickedness grows fiercer when it should hide; pricked by his words, they insult him instead.
Augustine: from his power learn patience — the Creator bore ‘thou hast a devil’ unretaliating.
Calvin: stupefied by Satan, convicted yet enraged; Henry: the men of consequence, beaten in argument, fall to railing.
He ignores the notorious ‘Samaritan’ slur, denies only ‘I have not a devil,’ and answers calumny with calm fact — ‘I honour my Father, and ye dishonour me’ — the opposite of demonic work.
Calvin: honouring the Father refutes the charge itself; in dishonouring Christ they dishonour the God they profess to defend.

Christ seeks not his own glory (5:41; 7:18) and so is unwounded by slander; the Father ‘seeketh and judgeth,’ vindicating the Son’s honour and sentencing his despisers (5:45) — only God-given glory is worth having.
To ‘keep my word’ (believe and obey) brings never seeing death (a Hebraism, Ps 89:48) — spiritual/eternal life now possessed (5:24; 11:25), beyond Moses’ promise of long life.
Calvin: a deliberate turn from terror to comfort, leaving consolation for the curable; the faithful teacher must also feed God’s children, not only reprove the wicked.
Henry: ‘the immortality of believers’ — the believer keeps Christ’s word in mind, love, and life.
Taking ‘never see death’ physically, they find it refuted by Abraham’s and the prophets’ deaths and so renew the demon charge; ‘now we know’ (perfect) and the shift to ‘taste of death’ noted.
Calvin: they wrongly apply the spiritual doctrine to the body — believers, born of incorruptible seed (1 Pet 1:23), ‘live even when they die.’
Gregory: the reprobate are made worse by kindness; Theophylact/Augustine read ‘I keep his saying’ of the Son’s unity with the Father.

Echoing the Samaritan woman on Jacob (4:12), they scornfully ask if he is greater than the (dead) Abraham and prophets; ‘whom makest thou thyself?’ half-suspects the blasphemy charged at 5:18 (later 10:33; 19:7).
Calvin: they obscure Christ’s glory with the saints’ splendour, but all the saints fade before him as stars before the sun.
Self-honour ‘is nothing’ (5:31); his glory comes from the Father (17:1) — the very God ‘ye say is your God,’ whom (v.55) they do not know.
Calvin/Henry: Christ ascribes all glory to the Father and thereby convicts the Jews of a hollow piety — claiming God while rejecting the Son he honours.

Their claim on God is hollow (‘ye have not known him’) while Christ knows the Father perfectly; to deny that knowledge would make him ‘a liar like you,’ so truth compels the claim — attested by his keeping the Father’s word.

themes: ‘ye have not known him; but I know him’

schools.summary: Abraham ‘rejoiced’ (ēgalliasato, leaping desire) to see Christ’s day from afar — by faith in the promise (Gen 22:18), in types/theophanies (Gen 18), perhaps the vision of Gen 15 (Heb 11:13); his exultation rebukes those who reject the Messiah present among them.
Calvin: Abraham longed for the absent Christ, they despise the present Christ; all saints, like the father of the Church, should rejoice in his presence.

Carnal literalism fixed on his age; they pervert ‘Abraham saw my day’ into ‘hast thou seen Abraham?’; ‘fifty’ is a round number of full manhood (Num 4:3), Jesus being about 33; a variant reads ‘has Abraham seen thee?’
Calvin: they try to refute him by impossibility, generously allowing him older years; he rejects Papias’ notion that Christ lived past forty.
Gregory: their carnal minds reckon only his age in the flesh, missing the eternity he claims.

The deliberate contrast of ‘came into being’ (genesthai, of Abraham) and the timeless ‘I am’ (egō eimi) is a claim to eternal, divine being — the absolute phrase used of God (Ps 90:2; 1:1), echoing the Name ‘I AM’ (Exod 3:14).
Calvin and Wesley: the plainest assertion of Christ’s eternal Godhead — he is before and above all time.

They take up stones because they heard a claim of deity (blasphemy, stoned per Lev 24:16) — confirming the sense of v.58; the temple under construction supplied the stones; Jesus ‘hid himself’ and passed through, his hour not come (7:30).

Augustine: ‘before Abraham was made’ (a creature) vs the Word who ‘was’ — he is, not ‘was made’; Gregory: minds unable to bear his eternity sought to destroy him; he withdraws not in fear but because his time was not yet.
